title: Tutorials
text:
How to Prepare for a Presentation

High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)








1778005667


Jamar Arrington

Attributions

Artwork from “Hello, Quarto” keynote by Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel and Julia Stewart Lowndes, presented at RStudio Conference 2022. Illustrated by Allison Horst.



A schematic representing the multi-language input (e.g. Python, R, Observable, Julia) and multi-format output (e.g. PDF, html, Word documents, and more) versatility of Quarto.





Two penguins in a room made of ice, looking out a window. One penguin is holding an icicle as a pointer, pointing at a Quarto logo “moon” in the sky, while the other penguin looks on.


For more from Allison, see her website and GitHub.

title: High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
section: Introduction: What Is High Blood Pressure?
text:
Introduction: What Is High Blood Pressure?

High blood pressure (hypertension) occurs when the force of blood against artery walls stays too high.
Often called the silent killer because it usually has no symptoms.
Normal BP: <120/80 mmHg

Hypertension: ≥130/80 mmHg
Major risk factor for heart disease, stroke, and kidney damage.

title: High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text:
Causes & Risk Factors

Lifestyle factors: high salt intake, inactivity, smoking, alcohol use.
Medical conditions: diabetes, kidney disease, high cholesterol.
Genetics & age: risk increases with age or family history.
Stress: chronic stress can elevate blood pressure.

title: High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text:
Effects on the Body

Damages blood vessels and strains the heart.
Increases risk of:

Heart attack
Stroke
Heart failure
Kidney disease
Vision loss

Early detection helps prevent long‑term complications.

title: High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
text:
Conclusion: Prevention & Management

Reduce salt intake and maintain a balanced diet.
Exercise regularly and maintain a healthy weight.
Avoid smoking and limit alcohol.
Monitor blood pressure consistently.
Follow medical guidance; medication may be needed.

title: SITENAME
text:
Preparing for a presentation becomes much easier when you follow a clear, intentional process. Good preparation helps you communicate confidently and keep your audience engaged from start to finish.


Decide the single takeaway you want your audience to remember. This central idea should guide your structure, examples, and visuals so everything feels focused and purposeful.



Think about what your audience already knows, what they care about, and what they hope to learn. Tailoring your tone and content to their needs makes your presentation more relevant and effective.



Use a simple, predictable format that keeps you organized: - An opening hook such as a question, story, or surprising fact - Two to four main points supported by examples or evidence - A closing statement or call to action that reinforces your message



Create slides that support your message rather than repeat it. Keep text minimal, use readable fonts, and rely on visuals like charts or images to highlight key ideas and maintain attention.



Rehearse out loud several times. Focus on pacing, transitions, and clarity. Recording yourself can help you notice habits you might miss in the moment, such as filler words or rushed explanations.



Test your equipment, check your slides on the actual screen, and make sure your notes are easy to access. A smooth setup reduces stress and helps you start strong.



List likely questions and prepare short, confident answers. This helps you stay composed during Q&A and shows strong command of your topic.
